text: Minimum Initial Service Package (MISP) for Reproductive Health in Crisis Situations

The MISP is a coordinated set of priority reproductive health activities to be implemented at the onset of every humanitarian emergency. It saves lives, prevents illness and disability, and lays the groundwork for comprehensive reproductive health care. The MISP was first developed in 1999 and has been revised and updated; the 2018 version reflects current evidence and field experience.
When to Implement the MISP

The MISP should be implemented immediately at the onset of any humanitarian emergency — including conflict, natural disaster, or complex emergency — before the situation has stabilised. It applies in refugee camps, displacement settings, and areas affected by sudden crisis. The MISP is not a preparedness plan; it is an immediate-response package for the acute phase.

The five MISP components must be implemented simultaneously. No single component can wait for others. Even when health staff are few and supplies limited, all five components should be started immediately, even in reduced form.
Component 1: Coordinate MISP Implementation

A lead reproductive health agency must be identified to coordinate MISP implementation across all responding organisations. The lead agency convenes reproductive health working group meetings, maps available services and gaps, advocates for resources, and ensures referral systems are functional. Without coordination, duplication and gaps in services are inevitable.

Key actions for the lead agency: (1) Identify and contact all RH service providers. (2) Conduct an initial RH assessment within 72 hours. (3) Set up a coordination mechanism (e.g., RH sub-cluster or working group). (4) Map referral pathways to EmOC facilities. (5) Establish a supply pipeline for RH commodities including IARH Kits.
Component 2: Prevent and Respond to Sexual Violence

Sexual violence — including rape, sexual exploitation, and abuse — increases dramatically during humanitarian emergencies. The MISP requires that clinical care for survivors of sexual violence be available from the first day of the emergency. This includes: post-exposure prophylaxis for HIV (PEP), emergency contraception, treatment of injuries, psychosocial first aid, and safe, confidential referral.

Clinical care for survivors must be provided within 72 hours of assault for PEP to be effective, and within 120 hours for emergency contraception. All health facilities should use a standardized intake form. Survivor confidentiality is paramount — medical records must be stored securely and information shared only with survivor consent.

Key actions: (1) Train all health staff in survivor-centered care. (2) Ensure IARH Kit 4 (Post-Rape Treatment Kit) is stocked at all primary health facilities. (3) Establish referral pathway from community to clinical care. (4) Implement security measures in camps to reduce risk (lighting, separate latrines for women, safe spaces). (5) Create a GBV referral network with psychosocial support services.
Component 3: Prevent HIV and STI Transmission

During humanitarian emergencies, HIV and STI risk increases due to displacement, transactional sex, sexual violence, and breakdown of health services. MISP actions focus on what can be implemented immediately: condom distribution, standard precautions for infection prevention, blood safety, and PEP for sexual violence survivors.

Key actions: (1) Ensure free condoms are widely available and promoted (IARH Kit 2). (2) Implement standard precautions for infection prevention in all health facilities — gloves, safe disposal of sharps, sterilization of instruments. (3) Ensure blood transfusion services screen all blood for HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and syphilis. (4) Make PEP available for sexual violence survivors within 72 hours. (5) Ensure health worker access to PEP after occupational exposure.

People living with HIV who are on antiretroviral therapy (ART) must be able to continue their treatment without interruption. The MISP requires that ART supply continuity be a priority — interruption of ART increases viral load, risks drug resistance, and endangers the person's health.
Component 4: Prevent Excess Maternal and Newborn Morbidity and Mortality

Approximately 4% of a displaced population will be pregnant at any time. In a population of 10,000 displaced persons, approximately 400 women will be pregnant, and around 20 will give birth each month. Without obstetric care, preventable maternal deaths — from hemorrhage, infection, obstructed labour, and eclampsia — will occur.

Key actions: (1) Distribute clean delivery kits (IARH Kit 0) to all pregnant women for home deliveries when health facility delivery is not possible. (2) Establish or designate at least one Basic Emergency Obstetric Care (BEmOC) facility per 500,000 population. (3) Establish at least one Comprehensive Emergency Obstetric Care (CEmOC) facility per 500,000 population with capacity for caesarean section. (4) Train midwives and skilled birth attendants. (5) Establish a functional referral system from community to EmOC.

The seven signal functions of Basic EmOC are: (1) administer parenteral antibiotics, (2) administer uterotonic drugs, (3) administer parenteral anticonvulsants, (4) manual removal of placenta, (5) removal of retained products, (6) perform assisted vaginal delivery, (7) perform basic neonatal resuscitation. CEmOC adds: (8) perform caesarean section, (9) perform blood transfusion.

Antenatal care should begin immediately, even in a simplified form: tetanus toxoid vaccination, iron and folic acid supplementation, assessment of gestational age, referral of high-risk pregnancies. Even a single ANC contact improves outcomes over no care.
Component 5: Plan for Comprehensive Reproductive Health Services

The MISP is an immediate response — not a long-term solution. From the first day of implementation, the lead RH agency must begin planning for the transition to comprehensive RH services as the situation stabilizes. This includes: family planning services, full antenatal care, safe abortion care (where legal), postnatal care, prevention and management of STIs including HIV, and services for adolescents.

Data collection must begin immediately. Key indicators to collect from day one: number of live births, maternal deaths, sexual violence cases treated, condoms distributed, family planning consultations. Data informs resource requests, identifies gaps, and demonstrates need to donors and coordination bodies.
MISP Commodities and Supply Pipeline

The INTER-AGENCY REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH (IARH) KITS are the primary supply vehicle for MISP implementation. They are pre-packaged, quality-assured sets of supplies designed to serve specific functions. The kits are organized by function, not by facility level. Key kits for MISP: Kit 0 (individual clean delivery), Kit 1 (facility clean delivery), Kit 2 (condoms), Kit 4 (post-rape treatment).

Ordering guidance: A population of 10,000 displaced persons requires approximately: 10 Kit 0 per month, 2 Kit 1 per month, 2 Kit 2 per month, 1 Kit 4 per quarter (replenish as used). These estimates are based on crude birth rates typical of humanitarian settings. Actual needs will vary — adjust based on population assessment.
Sexual and Reproductive Health in Adolescents During Crises

Adolescents (age 10–19) are a high-risk group in humanitarian settings. Girls face elevated risk of child marriage, sexual violence, and early pregnancy. Boys face risk of recruitment and sexual exploitation. The MISP includes adolescents in all activities. Specific considerations: friendly and confidential services, age-appropriate communication, peer education where feasible, and connection to safe spaces.

Pregnancy complications are the leading cause of death for girls aged 15–19 worldwide. Adolescent girls face higher risk of obstructed labour, obstetric fistula, and maternal death than adult women. They must have access to skilled birth attendance and EmOC without discrimination.
Coordination with Other Sectors

MISP implementation requires coordination with multiple sectors: (1) WASH — sanitation facilities must be separate by sex and safely lit; water supply supports handwashing and infection prevention. (2) Nutrition — severely malnourished pregnant women face higher obstetric risk. (3) Protection — GBV prevention requires joint action with the protection cluster. (4) Logistics — supply chain for commodities, cold chain for vaccines. (5) Mental health — psychosocial support for survivors of sexual violence is integral to care.
